food_subgroup: Rice noodle set meal with protein

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Mee Siam or dry Mee Siam with chicken wing. FOODON_03302051 (rice noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (rice noodles, cooked) is used as the closest parent. No specific FoodEx2 term exists for Mee Siam or this set meal combination.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Mee Siam or this specific Singaporean noodle dish set.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Dry Mee Siam with Chicken Wing Set is approximately 450 g, comprising a plate of dry rice vermicelli noodles with spice paste, one chicken wing, and one hard-boiled or fried egg.

nutrient_source.title: Mee Siam (dry), cooked — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo provides nutrient data for Mee Siam (dry) as a base dish. The chicken wing component is supplemented using HPB data for fried chicken wing. Per-100g values are weighted composites reflecting the full set meal (approximately 300 g noodle component + 120 g chicken wing + 30 g egg). Values represent the blended per-100g profile of the complete set.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodles, cooked (analogous estimate for dry Mee Siam base)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Dry Mee Siam with Chicken Wing Set. GI of 53 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked rice noodles (GI ~53, low category), which forms the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. The spice paste, protein, and fat components of the set meal are expected to moderate the overall GI further. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (53 × 83.25) / 100 ≈ 44.2, based on 450 g serving with 18.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from shrimp paste, dried shrimp, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1890 mg per 450 g serving), this dish provides a substantial portion of the 2000 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced seasoning and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index rice noodle base with significant carbohydrate load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: The dish has a GI of approximately 53 (low-moderate) but delivers a high glycemic load of approximately 44 per serving due to the large noodle portion; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the noodle portion, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and monitor postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from deep-fried chicken wing and sodium from spice paste
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-fried chicken wing contributes saturated fat (approximately 1.8 g per 100 g) and the shrimp-paste-based spice paste contributes high sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should opt for a grilled or steamed chicken alternative and request reduced seasoning to lower both saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dry Mee Siam with Chicken Wing Set is a Singaporean hawker-style set meal consisting of thin rice vermicelli noodles (bee hoon) stir-fried in a spicy, sweet, salty, and umami-rich spice paste (rempah), served dry rather than in broth, accompanied by one deep-fried or grilled chicken wing and one egg. The spice paste is typically made from dried shrimp, belacan (fermented shrimp paste), lemongrass, chilli, and tamarind, giving the dish its characteristic tangy-spicy flavour profile. The noodles are light and slightly chewy, coated in the aromatic paste, and often garnished with bean sprouts, tofu puffs, and a squeeze of lime. The chicken wing adds a crispy, savoury protein element, while the egg provides additional protein and richness. This dish is a popular breakfast and lunch option at Singaporean coffee shops (kopitiams) and hawker centres, and is commonly served at Toast Box outlets. Nutritionally, the set meal is a moderate-energy, carbohydrate-dominant meal with meaningful protein from the chicken wing and egg. The sodium content is high due to the shrimp paste and seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index of the rice noodle base is approximately 53 (low-moderate), but the large serving size results in a high glycemic load, which is relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is dairy-free and generally halal-certified when prepared with halal-certified ingredients.

confidence_reason: HPB Singapore Foodo provides nutrient data for Mee Siam (dry) as a base dish, and HPB data for fried chicken wing is used as a supplement. The per-100g values are a weighted composite estimate for the full set meal. No single HPB entry covers this exact set meal combination, so confidence is medium rather than high. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked rice noodles.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source database for this dish. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source database for this dish. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source database for this dish. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from HPB component data and USDA SR Legacy data for fried chicken wing. The cuisine field in the input JSON was listed as 'Toast' (referring to the Toast Box restaurant chain); this has been corrected to 'Singaporean' as the canonical cuisine classification. The food_group has been updated from 'Meat and alternatives' to 'Mixed' to reflect the composite nature of this set meal (noodles + protein + egg). Halal status is marked true based on Toast Box's general halal certification in Singapore, but consumers should verify at point of purchase. The glycemic load per serving is calculated based on a 450 g serving size with 18.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g: GL = (53 × 83.25) / 100 ≈ 44.2.

Calculated glycemic load per serving based on 450 g serving size.
